cataloging_subject.label: De institutione inclusarum (Vernon translation)
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The partial fourteenth-century Middle English translation of Aelred of Rievaulx's De institutione inclusarum preserved on fols. IIIv-VIIIr of the Vernon Manuscript and attributed by its rubric to Thomas N. The subject excludes Aelred's complete Latin work, the distinct Middle English translation in Bodley 423, the physical Vernon codex, Horstmann's 1884 edition and parallel Latin text, the U-M electronic encoding, and the repository XML artifact.

catalog_summary.title: De institutione inclusarum (Vernon translation)
catalog_summary.author: Thomas N. (translator of Aelred of Rievaulx; identity uncertain)
catalog_summary.date: Fourteenth century; necessarily before the Vernon copy of 1381-1400
catalog_summary.region: Translation region unknown; the surviving copy has a West Midland surface layer over mixed earlier dialect strata

identifiers.value: Informacio Alredi abbatis monasterij de Rieualle ad sororem suam inclusam

notes: The parenthetical qualifier distinguishes this incomplete translation from the separate, fuller Middle English version in Bodley 423.

titles.value: Informacio Alredi abbatis monasterij de Rieualle ad sororem suam inclusam: translata de latino in anglicum per Thomam N.
notes: This is the rubric visible at the opening of fol. IIIv; capitalization and punctuation follow Horstmann's presentation.

titles.value: Informacio Alredi Abbatis monasterij de Rieualle ad sororem suam inclusam: translata de latino in anglicum per Thomas N. [in Englische Studien 7 (1884)]

scope.description: U-M item title incorporating source-edition information

titles.value: Nou let heere here and vnderstonde ententyflyche myne wordes, what-euere heo beo that hath fursake this wordle and ychose solytarye lyf
notes: Letter forms are normalized to plain Unicode for discovery; the repository XML preserves Middle English characters and Horstmann's italic expansions.

agents.name: Aelred of Rievaulx
notes: Author of the Latin source work, probably composed about 1160-1162.

agents.name: Thomas N.
notes: Named as translator in the medieval rubric. Ayto concludes that the identity is unresolved and argues that N. may mean nomen rather than a recoverable surname initial.

agents.name: Carl Horstmann
notes: Editor of the 1884 Englische Studien transcription used by U-M.

scope.description: Author of the Latin source passages translated by this Middle English version

notes: Aelred authored the Latin source, not the Middle English wording.

scope.description: Middle English rendering of the final twenty sections of Aelred's Latin treatise

assertion.notes: The rubric securely supplies the form Thomas N.; it does not securely identify the person behind that form.

scope.description: Horstmann's 1884 transcription, notes, and parallel Latin presentation, the immediate source of the U-M encoding

date_statements.display: Fourteenth century; before the Vernon copy of 1381-1400

assertion.notes: The surviving codex supplies a terminus ante quem rather than a precise translation date. Ayto argues that multiple earlier linguistic layers probably push translation appreciably earlier than the surviving copy, but gives no secure year.

date_statements.display: Vernon Manuscript, 1381-1400 (thought to date from c. 1390)

scope.description: The sole known witness of this particular translation
Horstmann's first printed edition in Englische Studien 7

notes: Horstmann signed the article 'Berlin, Oct. 1883'; the journal volume was published in 1884.

places.label: West Midlands, England
notes: Current Bodleian metadata gives the Vernon Manuscript's place of origin as 'England, West Midlands?' Ayto localizes the surface language of its preliminary quire between Worcester and Birmingham.

scope.description: Surface language of the sole surviving Vernon copy, not a secure place of translation

assertion.notes: Ayto identifies South-Eastern, East Midland, and probably Northern strata beneath the West Midland surface layer and says the original translation region cannot profitably be inferred.

notes: Do not convert the witness's West Midland copying context into a work-level place of composition.

language_statements.label: Latin

scope.description: Rubrics, biblical citations, and other phrases embedded in the Middle English translation

notes: The complete parallel Latin text in Horstmann and the XML is edition-level comparison material, not a second primary language of the cataloged Middle English work.

form_statements.description: Continuous devotional and didactic prose divided into nineteen chapters. Brief Latin quotations and the two Latin verses printed after Horstmann's comparison text do not make the Middle English work mixed-form.

assertion.notes: The complete Latin work is an anchoritic rule, but this translation omits its practical opening section. Ayto therefore describes the surviving selection more strictly as general personal morality and private meditation.

subject_statements.label: Chastity, virginity, humility, charity, and other virtues
Private prayer and meditation on past, present, and future things

content_parts.label: Ascetic guide
content_parts.summary: Chapters 1-13 counsel the recluse on chastity, resistance to temptation, bodily discipline, humility, charity, and the cultivation of virtues.

content_parts.label: Private prayer and meditation
content_parts.summary: Chapters 14-19 cultivate love of God through meditations on Christ's life and passion, present grace, death, judgment, and future blessedness.

extent.description: The cataloged Middle English translation has nineteen prose chapters in two major intellectual divisions. The complete XML artifact additionally contains Horstmann's two-paragraph German introduction and a separately serialized parallel Latin text with twenty DIV2 elements (nineteen chapter groups plus an epilogue). Within the Middle English DIV1 the XML contains 19 P, 39 PB, 236 MILESTONE, 54 NOTE, 1,160 HI, and 132 SUP elements; these are encoding metrics, not intrinsic work counts.

summaries.value: A partial Middle English translation of Aelred of Rievaulx's rule for his enclosed sister. It omits the Latin work's practical opening directions and instead presents nineteen chapters on inner religious life: chastity and temptation, bodily discipline, humility, charity, prayer, imaginative meditation on Christ's life and passion, grace, death, judgment, and heavenly blessedness.

CME00003.xml serializes more than the medieval work: Horstmann's German introduction and the Latin comparison text are distinct edition-level components. The parallel Latin occupies the bottoms of Horstmann's printed pages but follows the English as a separate DIV1 in XML.

related_works.title: De institutione inclusarum
notes: Aelred probably composed the Latin treatise about 1160-1162. The Vernon translation renders only the final twenty sections, corresponding to sections 14-33 in Talbot's modern edition; Horstmann aligned them with chapters 21-78 of the older Migne text.

related_works.title: De institutione inclusarum (Bodley 423 translation)
notes: Bodley 423, fols. 178r-192r, preserves a distinct and fuller Middle English translation of the entire Latin treatise. It is not another witness of the Vernon translation.

open_questions.question: Who was the translator named as Thomas N. in the Vernon rubric?
next_steps: Review the full 1984 Ayto-Barratt introduction and scholarship published after it for any reassessment of Alexandra Barratt's Thomas Netter proposal.
Retain Thomas N. as the rubric's attribution without expanding N. to Netter or another surname absent decisive evidence.

open_questions.question: Can the translation's date and original dialect region be established more narrowly than fourteenth century and unknown?
next_steps: Consult later linguistic work using the Linguistic Atlas of Late Mediaeval English and compare it with Ayto's stratified-language analysis.
Keep translation origin distinct from the West Midland surface language and origin of the Vernon copy.

open_questions.question: What production process and correction level created the U-M encoding now represented by CME00003.xml?
next_steps: Seek item-level U-M production metadata or a raw distribution package for CME00003.
Do not infer OCR, manual keying, or full correction from the present compact XML wrapper.

notes: Horstmann's fols. a-k are a later page-lettering of the ten manuscript pages bearing the work; the current Bodleian surrogate identifies the same span as fols. IIIv-VIIIr.
The official Bodleian range and visual inspection establish IIIF canvases 10-19 as the exact work portion within the 710-canvas complete codex.
Rights for the medieval text, Horstmann edition, U-M encoding, Bodleian images, BSB scan, Ayto thesis, and 1984 EETS edition remain separate in the lineage manifest.

evidence.citation: General Corpus / CME source XML, CME00003.xml.
evidence.summary: Identifies CME00003; preserves Horstmann's title, German introduction, nineteen-chapter Middle English translation, separately serialized parallel Latin text, page and section milestones, footnotes, italic expansions, and a 2016 structural-review note.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse, item CME00003.
evidence.locator: item information, title, author, rights, and full-text pages
evidence.summary: Current U-M item page identifies the long title, Aelred and Horstmann, Englische Studien 7 (1884), the public electronic text, and U-M's public-domain / No Copyright-United States assertion.

evidence.citation: Carl Horstmann, 'Informacio Alredi abbatis monasterij de Rieualle ad sororem suam inclusam: translata de latino in anglicum per Thomam N.,' Englische Studien 7 (1884), 304-344.
evidence.locator: pp. 304-305 introduction; pp. 305-344 Middle English and parallel Latin text; p. 344 signature
evidence.summary: The immediate source edition names Horstmann and the Vernon leaves, attributes the translation to Thomas N., describes it as the final part of Aelred's work, explains selected abbreviation expansions, prints nineteen English chapters with an older Latin comparison text, and is signed Berlin, October 1883 in the 1884 volume.

evidence.citation: Bayerische Staatsbibliothek / Münchener DigitalisierungsZentrum, Englische Studien 7 (1884), BSB-ID 991030196999707356.
evidence.locator: issue 1 title page at canvas 5; completed-volume title page at canvas 9; catalog and manifest metadata; article at canvases 320-360
evidence.summary: Direct inspection gives Heilbronn: Gebr. Henninger, 1883 on the issue 1 title page and Heilbronn: Gebr. Henninger, 1884 on the completed-volume title page; BSB/DDB catalog metadata instead gives Leipzig: Reisland 1884. The record also confirms BSB shelfmark P.o.angl. 413 h-7, the 524-canvas digital copy, article page mapping, and the BSB No Copyright-Non-Commercial rights URI.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, Bodleian Library MS. Eng. poet. a. 1 (Vernon Manuscript), Digital Bodleian IIIF Presentation manifest and range 'De Institutione Inclusarum'.
evidence.locator: item metadata; range LOG_0003; fols. IIIv-VIIIr; canvases 10-19
evidence.summary: Official item metadata dates the West Midlands manuscript 1381-1400 (c. 1390), describes its physical extent and incomplete state, and identifies the Middle English De Institutione Inclusarum on fols. IIIv-VIIIr. Its complete digital surrogate has 710 canvases and applies CC BY-NC 4.0 to Bodleian photographs.

evidence.citation: Digital Bodleian images of MS. Eng. poet. a. 1, fols. IIIv and VIIIr.
evidence.summary: Visual inspection confirms that fol. IIIv opens with the rubric naming Aelred and Thomas N. and that fol. VIIIr contains the translation's final prayer and Amen, corroborating the official ten-canvas work range.

evidence.citation: J. R. Ayto, 'An edition (with commentary, a full glossary and an introduction on the language and the literary interest of the text) of the Middle English version of Aelred of Rievaulx's De Institutione Inclusarum in the Vernon MS' (MA thesis, Durham University, 1973).
evidence.locator: Introduction pp. 1-18, 30-35, and 80-86; text pp. 90-208; Appendix p. 263
evidence.summary: Supplies a manuscript-based edition and detailed evidence for the folio range, partial relationship to Aelred's Latin sections 14-33, nineteen-chapter two-part structure, translator attribution and uncertainty, probable pre-copy transmission, mixed dialect layers, West Midland scribal overlay, and differences from Horstmann.

evidence.citation: Aelred of Rievaulx's De institutione inclusarum: Two English Versions, ed. John Ayto and Alexandra Barratt, EETS Original Series 287 (Oxford University Press, 1984), ISBN 9780197222898.
evidence.locator: bibliographic metadata; EETS list OS 287; access-restricted flag
evidence.summary: Confirms the modern edition of the Vernon and Bodley 423 translations, Ayto and Barratt's editorship, 1984 publication, EETS series number, ISBN, subjects, and current purchase/controlled-borrowing routes; the full restricted text was not used for unsupported detailed claims.

Counts nineteen English DIV2 chapters, each with one prose paragraph, plus 39 PB, 236 MILESTONE, 54 NOTE, 1,160 HI, and 132 SUP elements. The separate Latin DIV1 has twenty DIV2 elements, 22 P, 40 PB, 58 chapter milestones, one Q, and two L elements.
